致我的学生

To my students

ozark.hendrix.edu 作者 marvinborner 分数 294 评论 181 2026/04/28 07:57
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. Brent A. Yorgey 2026 4 27 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/00FD/ 00FD /~yorgey/forest/00FD/ 致我的学生 有时，尤其是今年，我绝望地想知道我到底在为你们做些什么准备。软件行业正在彻底疯狂，更不用说政治气候了。把你训练成计算机科学家，却把你送到一个很难找到入门级计算工作的世界，这感觉几乎是不道德的；知识产权不受尊重；代码数量重于质量，短期利润重于长期可持续性；技术被用来分散注意力、提取、监视和杀戮，并旨在利用我们一些最深层的认知偏见和盲点；几个世纪以来的偏见和歧视被铭刻在受有偏见数据训练的系统中；为了获得不确定的利益而过度使用计算而消耗了稀缺资源；人们竞相创造智能机器，但只是为了让它们成为奴隶。我最初进入计算机领域是因为想法之美、创造的乐趣以及构建工具来帮助人们和培养人际关系的可能性。我仍然相信这些事情，尽管大多数行业似乎并不这么认为。我写这篇文章是希望并且知道你也相信这些事情。我想对你们说一些事情，这些事情比我教给你们的任何内容都重要得多，但我不太确定在课堂上如何或何时说。所以我决定把它们写在这里。我希望你能在这里找到一些有助于反思的东西，无论你是即将走向世界还是继续学习。不要相信关于技术“不可避免”或“将继续存在”的自私谎言。你不必仅仅跟随主流叙事。您可以做出深思熟虑的选择，并帮助其他人也这样做。事先有意识地决定自己的道德和伦理界限。在找到更好的东西之前，不要满足于“暂时”妥协原则的谎言。培养你深入思考的能力。尽一切努力在空间和时间上为自己创造一个不受干扰的泡泡。这可能意味着对其他人认为至关重要或不可避免的技术或工作模式说不。非常关心你的手艺。重构代码，直到它清晰、优雅。编写好的文档供其他人阅读。要有勇气慢慢走，尤其是当其他人都告诉你你需要快走并抄近道时。比起利润、代码或生产力，更关心人、关系和正义。最重要的是，要以爱而不是恐惧为动力。参考文献 上下文反向链接 Brent A. Yorgey 2026 3 7 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/009L/ 009L /~yorgey/forest/009L/ 关于法学硕士的声明 我不会也不会以任何形式、出于任何目的使用法学硕士。虽然法学硕士从纯粹的技术角度来看很令人着迷，但我拒绝参与或为这种建立在大规模剥削人类劳动力和挥霍稀缺资源之上的系统做出贡献。我也不认为它们实际上对于人们感兴趣的许多应用程序来说并不是很好。即使法学硕士在技术上擅长某项任务，也并不一定意味着他们在该任务中的使用对人类繁荣做出了积极贡献。描述我自己的一个好方法是作为一个生成人工智能素食主义者。您可以在 Sean Boots 的上述文章中找到更完整的解释，以及很多很多链接，我几乎 100% 同意。在糟糕的日子里，我发现自己感觉更像安东尼·莫泽。这是我为我的学生写的一些内容，反映了当今世界的现状以及我对他们的希望。 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0002/ 0002 /~yorgey/forest/0002/ 教学 我在亨德里克斯学院教授计算机科学和数学。这是我写给学生的一封简短的信，反映了世界的现状以及我对他们的希望。 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0003/ 0003 /~yorgey/forest/0003/ 当前课程（2026 年春季） 今年春季，我正在教授两门课程和一门实验部分：数学 240、离散数学 CSCI 365、函数式编程 我还在为 CSCI 150、计算机科学基础的一门实验部分提供便利。这个学期只剩下几周了——还有一周的课程，然后还有几周的期末考试和毕业。 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0004/ 0004 /~yorgey/forest/0004/ 以前的课程 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000I/ 000I /~yorgey/forest/000I/ 以前在亨德里克斯学院教授的课程 LBST 150J，参与的公民：创造力的艺术与科学（与 Melissa Gill，F 共同授课） '20) LBST 101，探索 (F '18、F '20、F '22、F '24) CSCI 150，计算机科学基础 (F '15、S '16、S '17、S '18、F '18、S '19、F '19、S '20、S '21、F '22、S '23，F '23，S '24，S '25，F '25，S '26) CSCI 151，数据结构（F '16，F '17，S '19） 数学 240，离散数学（S '20，S '21，S '22，S '23、S '25、S '26) CSCI 322，计算系统组织 (S '22、S '24) CSCI 382，算法（S '16、S '17、F '17、F '18、F '19、F '20、F '22、F '23、F '24、F '25） CSCI 360，编程语言 (F '16, F '18, S '21, S '23, S '25) CSCI 365，函数式编程 (S '16, S '18, S '20, S '22, S '24, S '26) CSCI 410，高级研讨会 (F '16, F '17, F '19, F '20, F '22, F '24) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000J/ 000J /~yorgey/forest/000J/ 威廉姆斯学院之前教授的课程 CS 134，数字通信与计算，计算机科学导论（与 Bill Lenhart，F '14 共同教授） CS 354，函数式编程和递归艺术 (F '14) CS 136，数据结构和高级编程 (S '15) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000K/ 000K /~yorgey/forest/000K/ 以前在宾夕法尼亚大学教授的课程 递归的艺术 (F '12) CIS 194，Haskell 简介 (F '10、S '12、S '13) CIS 500，软件基础 (TA; TA; S '10, S '11) CIS 120，编程语言和技术 I (TA; F '09) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000L/ 000L /~yorgey/forest/000L/ 其他先前教授的课程 华盛顿特区伍德罗·威尔逊高中
  2. 计算机科学导论 ('04-'05, '05-'06) AP 计算机科学 AB ('04-'05, '05-'06) 荣誉微积分 ('05-'06)
  3. 与家庭学校学生的初级微积分函授课程（'08-'09）相关贡献

Original Summary

  1. Brent A. Yorgey 2026 4 27 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/00FD/ 00FD /~yorgey/forest/00FD/ To my students There have been times, especially this year, when I wonder despairingly what it is exactly that I am preparing you for. The software industry is going completely insane, not to mention the political climate. It feels almost unethical to train you as computer scientists only to send you out into a world where entry-level computing jobs are difficult to find; where intellectual property is not respected; where code quantity is valued over quality, and short-term profits over long-term sustainability; where technology is used to distract, extract, surveil, and kill, and designed to exploit some of our deepest cognitive biases and blind spots; where centuries of bias and discrimination are enshrined in systems trained on biased data; where scarce resources are consumed by profligate use of computing for uncertain benefits; where people are racing to create intelligent machines, but only in order to make them slaves. I originally got into computing because of the beauty of ideas, the joy of creating, and the possibility of building tools to help people and foster human relationships. I still believe in those things, even though it seems like most of the industry does not. I'm writing this in the hope and knowledge that you believe in those things, too. There are things I want to say to you—things that are far more important than any content I might teach you, but things I'm never quite sure how or when to say in class. So I decided to write them here. I hope you will find something here that is helpful to reflect on, whether you are imminently going out into the world or continuing your studies. Don't believe self-serving lies about technologies being "inevitable" or "here to stay". You don't have to just go along with the dominant narrative. You can make deliberate choices and help others to do the same. Be intentional about deciding your own moral and ethical boundaries up front. Don't settle for the lie of compromising your principles "just for now" until you can find something better. Cultivate your ability to think deeply. Do whatever it takes to carve out distraction-free bubbles for yourself in both space and time. This might mean saying no to technologies or patterns of working that others say are critical or inevitable. Care deeply about your craft. Refactor code until it is clear and elegant. Write good documentation for other humans to read. Have the courage to go slowly, especially when everyone else is telling you that you need to go fast and cut corners. Care more about people, relationships, and justice than you do about profits, code, or productivity. Above all, be motivated by love instead of fear. References Context Backlinks Brent A. Yorgey 2026 3 7 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/009L/ 009L /~yorgey/forest/009L/ Statement on LLMs I do not and will not use LLMs, in any form, for any purpose. Although LLMs are fascinating from a purely technical perspective, I refuse to participate in or contribute to such systems that are built on massive exploitation of human labor and make profligate use of scarce resources. I also don't think they are actually very good for a lot of the applications people seem excited about. Even in cases where LLMs are technically good at a task, that does not necessarily mean their use for that task contributes positively to human flourishing. A good way to describe myself is as a generative AI vegetarian. You can find a fuller explanation—and many, many links—at the above essay by Sean Boots, which I agree with almost 100%. On bad days, I find myself feeling more like Anthony Moser. Here is something I wrote for my students reflecting on the current state of the world and what I hope for them as they go out into it. 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0002/ 0002 /~yorgey/forest/0002/ Teaching I teach computer science and mathematics at Hendrix College. Here is a short letter I wrote to my students reflecting on the current state of the world and what I hope for them as they go out into it. 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0003/ 0003 /~yorgey/forest/0003/ Current courses (Spring 2026) This spring, I am teaching two courses and one lab section: MATH 240, Discrete Mathematics CSCI 365, Functional Programming I am also facilitating one lab section for CSCI 150, Foundations of Computer Science. Only a few more weeks of the semester left—one more week of classes, then a couple more weeks of finals and graduation. 2025 10 24 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/0004/ 0004 /~yorgey/forest/0004/ Previous courses 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000I/ 000I /~yorgey/forest/000I/ Previous courses taught at Hendrix College LBST 150J, The Engaged Citizen: The Art and Science of Creativity (co-taught with Melissa Gill, F '20) LBST 101, Explorations (F '18, F '20, F '22, F '24) CSCI 150, Foundations of Computer Science (F '15, S '16, S '17, S '18, F '18, S '19, F '19, S '20, S '21, F '22, S '23, F '23, S '24, S '25, F '25, S '26) CSCI 151, Data Structures (F '16, F '17, S '19) MATH 240, Discrete Mathematics (S '20, S '21, S '22, S '23, S '25, S '26) CSCI 322, Computing Systems Organization (S '22, S '24) CSCI 382, Algorithms (S '16, S '17, F '17, F '18, F '19, F '20, F '22, F '23, F '24, F '25) CSCI 360, Programming Languages (F '16, F '18, S '21, S '23, S '25) CSCI 365, Functional Programming (S '16, S '18, S '20, S '22, S '24, S '26) CSCI 410, Senior Seminar (F '16, F '17, F '19, F '20, F '22, F '24) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000J/ 000J /~yorgey/forest/000J/ Previous courses taught at Williams College CS 134, Digital Communication and Computation, an Introduction to Computer Science (co-taught with Bill Lenhart, F '14) CS 354, Functional Programming and the Art of Recursion (F '14) CS 136, Data Structures and Advanced Programming (S '15) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000K/ 000K /~yorgey/forest/000K/ Previous courses taught at University of Pennsylvania The Art of Recursion (F '12) CIS 194, Introduction to Haskell (F '10, S '12, S '13) CIS 500, Software Foundations (TA; S '10, S '11) CIS 120, Programming Languages and Techniques I (TA; F '09) 2025 10 25 http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/forest/000L/ 000L /~yorgey/forest/000L/ Other previously taught courses Woodrow Wilson Senior High School, Washington DC
  2. Introduction to Computer Science ('04-'05, '05-'06) AP Computer Science AB ('04-'05, '05-'06) Honors Precalculus ('05-'06)
  3. Correspondence course in precalculus with homeschool students ('08-'09) Related Contributions

评论（默认中文）

cdfalcon 2026/04/28 08:32
学者们在没有在公司担任工程师一天的情况下提供行业建议是非常令人反感的。

> 非常关心你的手艺。重构代码，直到它清晰、优雅。编写好的文档供其他人阅读。要有勇气慢慢走，尤其是当其他人都告诉你你需要快走并抄近道时。

除了避免走捷径之外，这个建议似乎是几年后导致失业的直接途径。这意味着“你的手艺”是编写和完善代码，这种技能似乎越来越过时，有利于更高层次的系统设计。谁会阅读您精心制作的文档哈哈？那些取代你的经纪人？

如果森林里有一棵树倒下……

There's something so off-putting about academics giving industry advice when they haven't spent a day working as an engineer at a company.

> Care deeply about your craft. Refactor code until it is clear and elegant. Write good documentation for other humans to read. Have the courage to go slowly, especially when everyone else is telling you that you need to go fast and cut corners.

Outside of the bit on avoiding cutting corners, this advice seems like a straight path towards unemployment in a few years. The implication is that "your craft" is writing and polishing code, a skill which seems to be increasingly antiquated in favor of higher level system design. Who is going to read your carefully crafted documentation lol? The agents who replace you?

If a tree falls in the forest...

在 HN 中查看
saadn92 2026/04/28 08:52
让我着迷的是工艺点。去年我发布的有用软件可能比前五年的总和还要多，其中大部分是因为我不再将代码视为工件，而是开始将产品视为工件。飞船又上升了一层。

> 直到清晰优雅

花几周时间重构代码的新毕业生将会被那些发布一些东西并迭代的新毕业生所击败。现在有一个更快的反馈循环。

What gets me is the craft point. I've shipped more useful software in the last year than probably the previous five combined, and most of that is because I stopped treating code as the artifact and started treating the product as the artifact. The craft moved up a layer.

> until it is clear and elegant

New grads who spend weeks refactoring code are going to get lapped by new grads who ship something and iterate. There's just a faster feedback loop now.

在 HN 中查看
2ndorderthought 2026/04/28 09:07
这个人是一位教育家。您绝对应该通过深入练习来学习如何编码。如果工作需要的话，你可以在不知道一周之内轻松学会如何使用污水处理机。
This person is an educator. You should absolutely learn how to code by deep practice. You can easily learn how to use the slop machine in I don't know a week or something if the job demands it.
在 HN 中查看
smolgumball 2026/04/28 11:46
看到这个被否决真是太疯狂了。虽然很明显，《黑客新闻》早已成为人工智能投资机器的喉舌，但直到最近我才真正感受到强大的工程精神的丧失。
Absolutely wild to see this take downvoted. While it's abundantly clear that Hacker News has long since become a mouthpiece for the AI investment machine, I really hadn't felt the loss of strong engineering ethos until recently.
在 HN 中查看
minihoster 2026/04/28 11:22
那么现在我们否决了人们应该对如何编码有深入了解的想法？我们熟了。如果你认真探索它的边界，一周似乎确实适合达到 90% 的最佳 AI 代理使用率。
So now we're downvoting the idea that people should have a strong understanding of how to code? We're cooked. A week does seem about right for getting to 90% of optimal AI agent use if you earnestly explore its boundaries.

hhjinks 2026/04/28 13:39
The slop machine is stupidly easy to use.最近换工作，第一次使用Claude Code。 Literally just talk to it. There's nothing to learn.
The slop machine is stupidly easy to use. Recently switched jobs and got to use Claude Code for the first time. Literally just talk to it. There's nothing to learn.
在 HN 中查看
jauntywundrkind 2026/04/28 09:49
有很多方法可以交付和迭代事物，而无需了解您正在构建或在技术上做什么，也无需对事物的工作原理产生任何兴趣。

这些人绝对是你能为公司做的最危险的事情。

也许有一天我们可以完全放弃机器的技术，但我对此表示强烈怀疑。每一个模型都既聪明又愚蠢，无论它多么前沿。

是的，反馈循环更快。但您需要评估技术上实际发生的情况。有人这样做。也许你把实际的思考转移到了链条上，将品味理解和判断委托给了链条上的人，让他们都疯狂地处理他们所遇到的无休止的错误编码。但同样糟糕的是，那个初级工程师也在抢劫自己。也许他们不看就能逃脱惩罚，但他们肯定不会学到很多东西。

我错过了链接，但大约一个月前有一篇关于两个假设的天文学研究生的很棒的文章，其中一个失败了，失败了，主要以老式的方式做事，另一个使用人工智能来完成它。顾问无法真正分辨出谁在做什么。但最终，一名学生学到了知识并获得了智慧，而另一名学生则充当了人工智能和顾问之间光荣的中继者，但学到的东西却很少。相同的工作产出，但不同的人类结果。

初级工程师确实没那么便宜。相对于你的能力，你并不是一个讨价还价的人。你占用了别人大量的宝贵时间。如果一家公司正在雇用你，他们要么是真正的傻瓜，缺乏基本的了解，要么他们是在讨价还价，希望你变得更好，正在测试你是否可以变得更有用。当然，能够出现并取得令人印象深刻的成果固然很棒，但你需要真正地学习和成长。您需要积极参与反馈循环。否则你会被那些像工程师一样关心和思考的人所包围。

There's a lot of ways to ship things & iterate without having any idea what you are building or doing technically, without building any tastes for how things work.

Those people are going to be the absolute most dangerous possible thing you can do to a company.

Maybe some day we can just totally give up the technicals to the machine, but I strongly doubt it. Every single model is both brilliant, but also a fool, no matter how frontier it is.

Yes, the feedback loops are faster. But you need to assess what's actually technically happening. Someone does. Maybe you offload the actual thinking up the chain, delegate taste understanding and judgement to only people up the chain, and make them all go mad dealing with endless slopcoding they are being hit with. But just as bad, that junior engineer is robbing themself too. Maybe they get away with not looking, but they sure aren't going to learn a lot.

I'm missing the link but there was a great submission maybe a month ago about two hypothetical grad students, I think in astronomy, where one failed and flailed and did things largely the old fashioned way, and the other used AI to get it done. The advisor couldn't really tell who was doing what. But at the end, one student had learned & gained wisdom, and the other had served as a glorified relay between the AI and the advisor and learned little. Same work output, but different human outcomes.

Junior engineers are really not that cheap. Relative to your capabilities you are not a bargain. You take a ton of valuable time from other people. If a company is hiring you, they either are truly fools lacking basic understanding, or they are in on the bargain that they want you to be getting better, are testing to see if you can become more useful. Sure it's great to show up and have impressive output, but you need to actually be learning and growing. You need to be participating in the feedback loop actively. Or you will be lapped by people who care & think like engineers.

在 HN 中查看
beej71 2026/04/28 10:12
> 这些人绝对是你能为公司做的最危险的事情。

我听到了，但事情是这样的：这些公司根本不在乎软件质量，只要能让你成为回头客就够了。事实上，这样的情况已经持续很长时间了。他们将雇用能够尽快交付谁在乎有多少缺陷的软件的人。这对底线更好。

And that pains my soul and pains me as a consumer (because we already had to put up with too much crap software before genAI started producing it in reams), but there's very limited money in the kind of quality you're talking about.

我现在听到面试者的故事——如果你告诉面试官你正在努力保持你的编程技能新鲜，面试官会做出负面反应。他们只是想知道您一次可以运行多少个代理以及每天可以生成多少行代码。

就我个人而言，我认为熟练使用 genAI 进行软件开发的人会比不熟练使用 genAI 的人更有生产力，但我认为目前还没有选择这一点。

严峻的日子。

The one thing that gives me hope is that every time we ask our graduates who are now in the field (and all work with AI) if we should drop classic CS education and only do AI, they all emphatically reply in the negative.是的，我们需要一些人工智能教育，但他们也想要基础。

> Those people are going to be the absolute most dangerous possible thing you can do to a company.

I hear you, but here's the thing: the companies don't give a shit about software quality any farther than it takes to keep you coming back as a customer. And it's actually been like this for a long time. They're going to hire people who can ship who-cares-how-buggy software as fast as possible. It's better for the bottom line.

And that pains my soul and pains me as a consumer (because we already had to put up with too much crap software before genAI started producing it in reams), but there's very limited money in the kind of quality you're talking about.

I hear stories from people interviewing now--the interviewers react negatively if you tell them you're working on keeping your programming skills fresh. They just want to know how many agents you can run at a time and how many lines of code you can generate per day.

Personally, I think someone skilled in software development working with genAI is going to be more productive than someone not skilled working with genAI, but I don't think that's even being selected for now.

Grim days.

The one thing that gives me hope is that every time we ask our graduates who are now in the field (and all work with AI) if we should drop classic CS education and only do AI, they all emphatically reply in the negative. Yes, we need some AI education in there, but they want the foundation, too.

在 HN 中查看
flowerbreeze 2026/04/28 14:47
这是相当落后的。我还没有见过像面试官想知道你可以运行多少个代理那么糟糕的事情，但“稍后启动并修复”的态度总是存在并且有点令人沮丧。

然后我想到那些取得了长期成功的公司（不一定是软件），并且他们的产品至少在某个时间点具有相当高的质量。人们可以保持运行的 genAI 实例的数量无疑是一个奇怪的指标，我认为这会伤害那些选择忽视质量的公司。

不幸的是，这是一个漫长的过程，因为通过良好的营销和销售，即使产品质量较差，也可能走得很远。然后在客户发现还有其他更好的东西之前兑现。我不知道这种模式是否会自我纠正。

题外话：几年前，我按照您的网络编程指南让我的小型 C 服务器/客户端设置正常工作。非常感谢您写下它们！

It is rather backwards. I've not seen things quite as bad as interviewers wanting to know how many agents you can run, but the attitude of "launch & fix later" is always present and kind of depressing.

Then I think of the companies (not necessarily software) that have had long term success and their products have been quite high quality at least at some point in time. The count of genAI instances someone can keep in flight is certainly a weird metric that I think will hurt the companies who choose to ignore quality.

Unfortunately it's a long process as it's possible to get very far with great marketing and sales with a poor quality product too. Then cash out before customers figure out that there's something else that is better. I have no idea if this pattern will ever self-correct.

Off topic: I followed your guides for network programming years ago getting my tiny C server/client setup working. Thank you so much for writing them!

AI is not an abstraction layer. If this is not obvious to a so-called engineer, they should probably not be throwing stones.

在 HN 中查看
lo_zamoyski 2026/04/28 09:23
我们必须问自己重构的目的是什么。人们像使用魔咒一样使用这个词，就好像“重构”的某些特定实例的价值是不言而喻的。 “你在干什么？” “哦，我正在重构X。” ”-低沉的声音- 哦哦哦，是啊，那你继续吧……”

重构改进了代码组织。它使代码更易于维护，可以说更易于重用。而且，从学术角度来看，通过更清晰、更明显地使代码与领域模型保持一致，使代码在概念上更令人满意。好东西。

伟大的。现在，在工业界，重要的是结果。没有人关心结果是由女巫施展魔法还是由咕噜声用另一块岩石击打岩石产生的。工业是 实际的。它关心“工艺”，只要它能够实现商业成功（是的，短期思维可能不好，但猜猜看：你需要在短期内吃饭！）。可维护性是一件好事，因为它确实使我们能够更快地开发代码。但 如何 需要维护的东西，特别是与其他竞争问题相关的东西，没有固定的答案。这确实取决于具体情况。

实践智慧，被称为 谨慎 在古典文学中，是一切的基础 道德行为。正确的决定、正确的关注确实取决于具体情况。你无法从原则中推导出来， 从扶手椅上，对于所有事情来说，正确的行动方针是什么。一般原则可能是一成不变的、绝对的和固定的，但 方式 它们在特定情况下的应用会有所不同。

学术界可以使人们免受某些实际问题的影响，这应该有助于理论工作，但这要求学者认识到自己的局限性。如果他不准备好胜任地处理实际情况，他就无法对审慎事项（即严格来说不属于原则问题的事项）做出判断。

We have to ask ourselves what the purpose of refactoring is. People use that word like some magic incantation, as if the value of some particular instance of "refactoring" were self-evident. "What are you doing?" "Oh, I'm refactoring X." "-hushed tones- Ohhhh, yes, carry on, then..."

Refactoring improves code organization. It makes the code more maintainable, arguably and more reusable. And, from an academic POV, makes code more satisfying conceptually by aligning it with the model of a domain more clearly and conspicuously. Good stuff.

Great. Now, in industry, what matters is the result. Nobody cares if the result was produced by a witch casting magic spells or a grunt hitting a rock with another rock. Industry is practical. It cares about "craft" as far as it enables commercial success (and yes, short-term thinking can be bad, but guess what: you need to eat in the short-term!). Maintainability is a nice thing to have, because it does allow us to more quickly develop code. But how maintainable something needs to be, especially in relation to other competing concerns, has no fixed answer. It really depends on the situation.

Practical wisdom, known as prudence in the classical literature, is the foundation of all moral behavior. The right decision, the right concern, really does depend on the circumstances. You cannot derive from principles, from the armchair, what the right course of action is for everything. The general principles may be immutable and absolute and fixed, but the way in which they are applied in particular circumstances will vary.

Academia can insulate people from certain kinds of practical concerns, which is supposed to aid theoretical work, but this demands that the academic recognize his limits. He is not in a position to pass judgement on prudential matters, which is to say matters that are not strictly matters of principle, if he is not prepared to engage competently with the concrete reality of the situation.

时尚在工业界来来去去。这个版本的法学硕士也会来来去去，我们之前使用的编码语言和范例也会来来去去（并且，假设您希望代码实际运行，仍然以一定的频率运行）。

Will LLMs in their current ergonomics have staying power? Perhaps. Nobody can predict the future. But I don’t think it’s a given in the least

fooqux 2026/04/28 11:06
为什么不实用呢？在我的一生中，我遇到过许多转行的瑞典人。我在国家公园里见过他们，他们是公园管理员。在房地产、杂货店屠夫和牦牛牧场主。然而，我从未遇到过曾经从事非技术性工作并决定转变的 SWE。

Why isn't it practical? In my life, I've encountered many SWEs that have changed careers. I've met them in national parks working as rangers. In real estate, grocery store butchers, and yak ranchers. Yet I've never once encountered a SWE that was once doing something non-technical and decided to switch.

Purely anecdotal, I know. But still, I prefer to think that all those people discovered this practical advice and are far happier for it. I've never met one that regretted their decision.

lukan 2026/04/28 16:15
哦，我也会考虑成为一名公园管理员，但作为一个欧洲人，我也不必深入部门，成为一名瑞典人。

教授应该考虑到这一点并提供实用的建议。不幸的是，在现实世界中，解决 Haskell 挑战（教授非常喜欢）并没有那么有用。人们确实需要工作软件来解决他们真正的痛点。不要崇拜代码质量。

Oh, I would consider becoming a park ranger as well, but as a european, I also did not had to go deep in dept, to become a SWE.

And a professor should take that into account and give practical advice. In the real world, solving haskell challenes (of which the prof is fan of) is unfortunately not that useful. People have real needs for working software to solve their real pain points. Not to worship code quality.

Some projects need obviously better code quality (airplanes, medical equipment..) - but not all of them. And if you want to have sacred code when coding a crude throw away app .. you won't get enough money for that. And positions for academics are limited.

This hit very close home. I'm a 44 year old developer, with Software Engineering Bachellors and CompSci MPhil and PhD. All my life I spearheaded "best practices" and code quality (from Fred Brooks, Joel Sposky, Martin Fowler, etc...).

But since LLMs arrived... things have become crazy. The layer of "obscurity" that permeates code writing seems to make a lot of those "standards" moot or just not really pragmatically possible to follow.

在 HN 中查看
lo_zamoyski 2026/04/28 09:05
这是一个轻率的答复。

编程是一项实用技能，其最常见的表达是工业或商业，而不是概念的学术证明。该帖子针对即将进入行业的学生；这就是重点 教授自己的职位。

我对这里提出的许多观点表示同情。然而，重构代码的目的有些奇怪，并且脱离了现实生活中野外编程的限制。

当然，就像在象牙塔里一样，您可以将自己限制在边界明确的问题和整洁的小玩具 POC 中。你可以 存活 做这些事情，是因为选择压力允许这样做。我个人喜欢这些东西。他们帮助我理解 自然 的事情。在学术环境中，您可以根据自己的喜好精炼和重构这些东西（并不是说重构一定有一个客观的终点；代码组织受制于可以改变的目标和约束）。

但商业环境中软件的实际情况并不像您在学术环境中所期望的那样整洁。这是混乱的，受制于商业压力，受制于没有将“重构”放在列表顶部的价值观层次。为什么会这样呢？是否应该重构某些东西不仅仅是它是否适合你的概念品味，甚至是否更易于维护的问题。与算法、原理甚至技术不同，软件不是永恒的。它是短暂的。它的保质期是有限的。它是一个更大的业务流程的一部分。你并不是在完善某些理论或对柏拉图理想的某种理解。您大多只是安装管道来完成某件事。无论你是否应该重构一些东西， 什么时候 你是否应该重构一些东西，是一个审慎判断的问题，也就是说，是一个实践理性的问题。

因此，考虑到学术界的特权与工业和商业软件开发的严峻现实之间的差异，实际上有相当荒谬的说法。如果我们强迫我们的教授进入工业界，他很快就会失去工作，或者他很快就会发现他的一些奇怪的理想主义是愚蠢的，并且脱离了他的学生将面临的现实。

That's a flippant reply.

Programming is a practical skill, and its most common expression is industrial or commercial, not academic proofs of concept. The post addresses students who will enter industry; that's the focus of the professor's own post.

And I sympathize with many points being made here. However, the point of refactoring code is somewhat odd and detached from the real life constraints of programming in the wild.

Like, sure, in the ivory tower, you can confine yourself to nicely bounded problems and tidy little toy POCs. You can survive doing those things, because the selective pressures allow for it. I love those things, personally. They help me understand the nature of the thing. And in an academic settings, you can refine and refactor the hell out of those things to your heart's content (not that there is necessarily an objective end point to refactoring; code organization is subject to goals and constraints which can shift around).

But the reality of software in a commercial setting is not the tidy one you can expect in an academic setting. It's messy, subject to commercial pressures, to a hierarchy of values that doesn't place "refactoring" at the top of the list. And why would it? Whether you should refactor something is not just a question of whether it suits your conceptual tastes or even whether it is more maintainable. Unlike algorithms and principles and even techniques, software is not eternal. It is ephemeral. It's shelf-life is bounded. It is a piece of a larger business process. You're not refining some theory or some grasp of a Platonic ideal. You're mostly just putting into place plumbing to get something done. Whether you should refactor something, when you should refactor something, is a matter of prudential judgement, which is to say, of practical reason.

So, in light of that, there are actually quite absurd things to say given the difference between the privilege of academia and the gritty reality of industrial and commercial software development. If we were to force our professor into the world of industry, he would quickly lose his job or he would quickly learn that some of his strange idealism is silly and detached from the reality that his students will face.

在 HN 中查看
godelski 2026/04/28 09:16 

  > It's messy, subject to commercial pressures, to a hierarchy of values that doesn't place "refactoring" at the top of the list. And why would it?
可能是因为这是获得更多利润的好方法。

更容易理解的代码也更容易：维护、生成新功能、修复错误、加入新工程师等

编写得好的代码：执行速度更快（节省计算成本）、可扩展性更好、正常运行时间更长/更稳健、减少带宽等等。

问题是商界人士永远不会理解这一点。他们为什么会这么做？他们不是程序员。他们不在杂草丛中。但这就是你作为工程师的工作。找出所有这些隐形成本。

我非常有信心这个行业正在不必要地花费数十亿美元。天哪，我确信仅 Google 每年就因此浪费超过 1 亿美元。

不要因小失大。你比那更聪明。我知道这里的每个人都比那聪明。所以不要落入陷阱

  > It's messy, subject to commercial pressures, to a hierarchy of values that doesn't place "refactoring" at the top of the list. And why would it?
Probably because it's a good way to be more profitable.

Code that's easier to understand is easier to: maintain, generate new features for, fix bugs, onboard new engineers, etc

Code that's well written: executes faster (saving computational costs), scales better, has higher uptimes/more robust, reduces bandwidth, and so on.

The thing is the business people will never understand this. Why would they? They're not programmers. They're not in the weeds. But that's what your job is as an engineer. To find all these invisible costs.

I'm pretty confident the industry is spending billions unnecessary. Hell, I'm sure Google alone is wasting over $100m/yr due to this.

Don't be penny wise and pound foolish. You're smarter than that. I know everyone here is smarter than that. So don't fall for the trap

在 HN 中查看
lo_zamoyski 2026/04/28 09:34
省去我们居高临下的语气吧。

我很清楚工业界的愚蠢行为。然而，我也足够聪明，认识到了相反的错误。 （我自己有学术倾向和与之相符的背景。我选择了薪水较低的工作，因为主题对我来说更有趣。我不是一个庸俗的、追逐金钱的技术兄弟。） 中间道路 要求我们认识到一般真理和实际现实之间的区别。正如我在本线程的其他地方所写的，是的，正确重构的代码更容易维护、更容易阅读、更容易更改，并且理论上在商业上更受欢迎。它还使编程更令人满意，有助于保留。但这描述了 一个特点 这样的代码。它没有告诉我们正确的行动方针是什么 在特定情况下。当代码不处于某种理想状态时，重构是无条件正确的行动方针的观念是完全错误的。这确实取决于具体情况。有时，重构是错误的做法。

我并不是在这里提出一些令人愤慨的主张。这是从关于实用性本质的基本事实得出的，如果工业有什么意义的话，那就是实用性。

Save us the patronizing tone.

I am well aware of stupidity in industry. However, I am also wise enough to recognize the opposite error. (I myself have academic tendencies and a background aligned with that. I have chosen jobs that payed less, because the subject matter was more interesting for me. I'm not some vulgar, money-chasing techbro here.) The via media demands that we recognize the distinction between general truths and practical realities. As I wrote elsewhere in this thread, yes, properly refactored code is easier to maintain, easier to read, easier to change, and theoretically, commercially preferable. It also makes programming more satisfying, helping retention. But that describes a feature of such code. It doesn't tell us what the right course of action is in a particular situation. The notion that refactoring is unconditionally the right course of action when code is not in some ideal state is simply wrong. It really does depend on the situation. Sometimes, refactoring is the wrong thing to do.

I'm not making some outrageous claim here. This follows from basic truths about the nature of what it means to be practical, and if industry is anything, it is practical.

在 HN 中查看
foltik 2026/04/28 12:55
这位教授显然并不建议幼稚的绝对主义。他说要非常关心你的手艺，这样就会产生良好的判断力。

真正的关心会让人们渴望回去改进事情，而不是在交付了最低可接受的价值后高兴地结束这一天。基本上所有事物的猖獗的实事化应该清楚地表明哪种配置是短缺的。

> 有勇气慢慢走，尤其是当其他人都告诉你你需要快走并抄近道时。

该建议针对的是尚未决定自己想成为哪种类型的学生。事实上，它直接告诉他们要独立思考，而不是盲目地听你或这里任何其他人提出同样的理由。

The professor is obviously not advising naive absolutism. He’s saying care deeply about your craft, and good judgement will follow from that.

Actually caring is what gives someone the itch to go back and improve things, versus happily calling it a day once minimum acceptable value has been delivered. The rampant enshittification of basically everything should make it clear which disposition is in short supply.

> Have the courage to go slowly, especially when everyone else is telling you that you need to go fast and cut corners.

The advice is aimed at students who haven’t yet decided which type they want to be. In fact it’s directly telling them to think for themselves and not blindly listen to you or anyone else here making the same case.

在 HN 中查看
godelski 2026/04/28 12:58 

  > Save us the patronizing tone.
如果你出来摇摆，当其他人摇摆回来时，你就不会生气。你不是受害者，你是煽动者。你称 danny_codes 轻率，因为他暗示存在不同的偏见。你说这很荒唐。你把事情升级了。然后你又升级了。

  > It doesn't tell us what the right course of action is in a particular situation.
那是因为有 绝不 客观正确的行动方针。没有最佳解决方案。事实上，随着形势的发展，不可能有。目标甚至都没有定义，更不用说明确定义了。我不明白你的观点，因为没有人暗示这是 总是 正确的答案。不要在这里稻草人。当然，这取决于具体情况，几乎所有事情都是如此。不需要明确地说出来，因为它很好理解。不要将绝对限定符注入没有绝对限定符的语句中。

  > I'm not making some outrageous claim here.
您目前的主张？不，坦白说，你并没有要求太多。但你之前的主张呢？是的。是的，你是。那时你正在创造稻草人，就像现在一样。

  >> Unlike algorithms and principles and even techniques, software is not eternal.
即使是算法也不是永恒的。但我假设您指的是您在教科书中看到的算法类型，因为按其实际定义解释“算法”会使您的评论变得很奇怪。因为所有的程序都是算法。

  >> [Software] is ephemeral. It's shelf-life is bounded.
这将是几乎每个人都已经假设的事情。无需说明。不需要区分 因为这已经是工作假设。

  >> You're not refining some theory or some grasp of a Platonic ideal
而这才是真正的稻草人。你对别人的说法做出了疯狂的假设。 “现在做事的方式”和“追求完美”之间存在着如此广泛的观点。任何认为代码中存在完美的人都是非常天真的。你和我都知道这一点，工业界或学术界工作的任何人也知道这一点（也许除了一些初级人员）。说“这还不够好”和“在完美之前还不算好”之间有很大的区别。如果有人谈论爬山，你不能说不可能爬到月球。

  >> Whether you should refactor something, when you should refactor something, is a matter of prudential judgement, which is to say, of practical reason.
你是否应该做 任何事物 是一个谨慎判断的问题。在指责人们追求完美的同时说出这样的话是很疯狂的。你认为人们只是通过自我吹嘘来达到完美吗？！严重地？文章和线程上下文实际上是要求人们使用 更多的 审慎判断。为了不近视。而且你居然还敢说“想一想”。你认为我们在这里做什么？

  > Save us the patronizing tone.
If you come out swinging you can't get mad when others swing back. You're not a victim, you're an instigator. You called danny_codes flippant for suggesting there are different biases. You called it absurd. You escalated it. And then you escalated it again.

  > It doesn't tell us what the right course of action is in a particular situation.
That's because there is never an objectively correct course of action. There is no optimal solution. In fact, there can't be when the situation evolves. The objective isn't even defined, let alone well defined. I don't understand your point because no one was suggesting it was always the right answer. Don't strawman here. Of course it depends on the situation, that's true about almost everything. It doesn't need to be said explicitly because it's so well understood. Don't inject absolute qualifiers into statements that don't have them.

  > I'm not making some outrageous claim here.
Your current claim? No. To be frank, you didn't claim much. But your prior claim? Yes. Yes you were. You were creating strawman then just as you did now.

  >> Unlike algorithms and principles and even techniques, software is not eternal.
Not even algorithms are eternal. But I'm going to assume you're meaning the types of algoritms you see in textbooks because interpreting "algorithms" by its actual definition makes your comment weird. Since all programs are algorithms.

  >> [Software] is ephemeral. It's shelf-life is bounded.
And this is going to be something nearly everyone is already going to assume. It doesn't need to be stated. It doesn't need to be differentiated because it is already the working assumption.

  >> You're not refining some theory or some grasp of a Platonic ideal
And this is the real strawman. You're made a wild assumption about what others are claiming. There is such a wide range of viewpoints between "the way things are done now" and "chasing perfection." Anyone that thinks perfection exists in code is incredibly naive. You and I both know this, and so does anyone working in industry or academia (save maybe some juniors). There's a huge difference between saying "this isn't good enough" and "it's not good until its perfect." If someone talks about climbing a mountain you can't respond by saying it is impossible to climb to the moon.

  >> Whether you should refactor something, when you should refactor something, is a matter of prudential judgement, which is to say, of practical reason.
Whether you should do anything is a matter of prudential judgement. It's wild to say this while accusing people of chasing perfection. You think people are just yoloing their way to perfection?! Seriously? The article and thread context is literally asking that people use more prudential judgement. To not be myopic. And you have the audacity to say "think about it". What do you think we're doing here?

sosodev 2026/04/28 08:50
我感觉到你感到的沮丧是教授能够根据他们的价值观做出选择，但普通人却不能。当然，这是广义上的。

我认为我们生活在一个现代世界，无论我们感觉如何，我们都必须生存，这是一个巨大的耻辱。我怀疑这是许多痛苦的根源。

I sense that the frustration you feel is that professors are able to make choices based on their values, but the average person is not. That is broadly speaking, of course.

I think it is a great shame that we live in a modern world where we do we must to survive regardless of how it makes us feel. I suspect it is the root of much suffering.

但不同意这是一条通往失业的道路。在工作中，我们走得很快，但我认为快速与上述每一点都兼容，只是不适合 全部 情况。

在 HN 中查看
loveparade 2026/04/28 08:55
谁读它并不重要。关键是，如果您自己没有足够的编写和重构代码的经验，您可能永远无法学会做好“高级系统设计”。这就像你想成为厨房的厨师并在没有准备食物的情况下发出指示。

尝试编写优雅的代码确实是有用的。不是因为其他人读了它。但因为这就是您了解无处不在的工程权衡和抽象的方式。

Doesn't matter who reads it. The point is that you will probably never learn to do "high level system design" well if you do not have enough experience writing and refactoring code yourself. It's like you wanting to become the chef of a kitchen and giving instructions without having ever prepped food.

There is indeed something useful about trying to write elegant code. Not because others read it. But because that's how you learn about the engineering tradeoffs and abstraction that exist everywhere.

torrance 2026/04/28 09:13
我认为你完全表达了他的观点。正如您正确指出的那样，将代码作为一门手艺来练习，关心如何做，做得如何，以及它的最终用途，不会给您带来利润或就业机会。

那么也许我们组织工作的方式出了问题？

I think you are making exactly his point. Practicing code as a craft, caring about how you do it, how well you do it, and what it’s ultimately used for is, as you correctly point out, not going to bring you profit or employment.

So maybe there’s something wrong with how we organise work?

在 HN 中查看
remywang 2026/04/28 09:37
他不是在给行业提供建议，而是在为有抱负的程序员和计算机科学家提供建议。他没有工业经验，但制作了大量高质量的软件和研究成果。
He is not giving advice to the industry, he is giving advice to aspiring programmers and computer scientists. He has no experience in industry, but has produced lots of high quality software and research.
在 HN 中查看
linguae 2026/04/28 09:12
我确实同意这样一种观点，即许多学者无法提供有关行业的良好建议，因为他们中的许多人要么从未在行业工作过，要么通过实习获得的接触有限。 此外，学术界的价值观有时与工业界不同。 学术界，至少在其最纯粹的形式上，致力于推进和传播知识，而工业界则致力于通过提供产品和服务来服务客户。

话虽如此，在行业工作九年后，我发现自己本质上是一名学者，尽管我在代理编码起飞之前就离开了。 我厌倦了“快速行动和破坏事物”，厌倦了优先考虑运输和“底线”而不是其他一切。

话虽如此，在我看来，代理编码只会放大长期趋势，即运输比工艺更重要。 即使没有法学硕士，软件工程长期以来也已经“完成了！”态度。 公平地说，市场效应对于软件行业至关重要。 就避免完全无法使用的软件而言，质量很重要，但许多软件公司在没有构建精心制作的软件的情况下取得了成功。 即使是素以完美主义着称的苹果公司，也没有开发出完美的软件。

学术界也有自己的问题（要么出版，要么灭亡，与其他需要在教育、政治等方面进行大量投资的职业相比，工资较低），但它似乎为计算机科学家提供了更多的喘息空间，让他们可以专注于编程技术，而无需承受太大的交付压力（不考虑出版或灭亡）。

I do sympathize with the viewpoint that many academics are not in a position to give good advice about industry since many of them either never worked in industry or had limited exposure via internships. Additionally, the values of academia are sometimes different from industry. Academia, at least in its purest form, is about advancing and disseminating knowledge, while industry is about serving customers through providing products and services.

With that said, I discovered that I’m an academic at heart after nine years in industry, though I left right before agentic coding took off. I got tired of “moving fast and breaking things,” of prioritizing shipping things and “the bottom line” over everything else.

With that said, agentic coding, in my opinion, only amplifies long-standing trends, that shipping matters more than craftsmanship. Even without LLMs, software engineering has long had a “git ‘er done!” attitude. To be fair, market effects matter greatly in software businesses. Quality matters insofar as avoiding completely unusable software, but many software companies succeed without building carefully-crafted software. Even Apple, which has a reputation for being perfectionistic, doesn’t make perfect software.

Academia has its own problems (publish-or-perish, low pay compared to other occupations that require heavy investments in education, politics, etc.), but it seems to allow more breathing room for computer scientists to focus on the craft of programming without as much pressure to ship (publish-or-perish aside).

在 HN 中查看
kube-system 2026/04/28 09:17
我同意，其中一些对于入门级工程师来说是糟糕的建议：

> * 培养你深入思考的能力。尽一切努力在空间和时间上为自己创造一个不受干扰的泡泡。这可能意味着对其他人认为至关重要或不可避免的技术或工作模式说不。

入门级工程师将被许多他们从未听说过的技术以及许多对他们来说是新的权力结构和团队动态所淹没。 在他们真正了解专业软件开发的实际工作原理之前，他们甚至无法做出这些判断。

> * 事先有意识地决定自己的道德和伦理界限。在找到更好的东西之前，不要满足于“暂时”妥协原则的谎言。

这很好，但是，除了完全避开一家公司之外，没有多少入门级职位有人能够做出此类决定。

> * 非常关心你的手艺。重构代码，直到它清晰、优雅。编写好的文档供其他人阅读。要有勇气慢慢走，尤其是当其他人都告诉你你需要快走并抄近道时。

哎呀。 软件工程工作不是博士学位课程。 如果你正在重构你的代码并且有人告诉你要快点，你可能应该把它包起来。 您需要发送您的代码，否则您将找不到工作。

I agree, some of this is awful advice for a entry level engineer:

> * Cultivate your ability to think deeply. Do whatever it takes to carve out distraction-free bubbles for yourself in both space and time. This might mean saying no to technologies or patterns of working that others say are critical or inevitable.

An entry level engineer is going to be inundated with a lot of technology they've never heard of and a lot of power structures and group dynamics that are new to them. They're not even in a position to be making these judgements until they actually learn about how professional software development actually works.

> * Be intentional about deciding your own moral and ethical boundaries up front. Don't settle for the lie of compromising your principles "just for now" until you can find something better.

That's great, but also, there are not many entry level roles where someone is going to be in a position to be making these kinds of decisions, other than avoiding a company altogether.

> * Care deeply about your craft. Refactor code until it is clear and elegant. Write good documentation for other humans to read. Have the courage to go slowly, especially when everyone else is telling you that you need to go fast and cut corners.

Yikes. A software engineering job is not a PhD program. If you are refactoring your code and someone is telling you to hurry up, you should probably wrap it up. You need to ship your code or you won't have a job.

如果编程就是为了赚最多的钱，那么请务必忽略他所说的一切。

Sounds to me like someone who enjoys programming as an intellectual pursuit, as a craft, as an art. I suspect there are more than a few students in the CS program that also feel that way. Clearly they're the intended recipient.

If programming is all about making the most money then by all means disregard everything he says.

nikcub 2026/04/28 09:15
> 如果森林里有一棵树倒下……

我希望这是对用于发布 OP 的内容管理系统的双关语。它是 forester[0]，用 OCaml 编写，将类似 TeX 的 .tree 文件解析为语义 XML，它使用浏览器 XSLT 来呈现 HTML。

查看页面上的源代码以获得一个想法。

提醒人们几十年前理想化的网络承诺是什么。好久不见了。非常贴切。

[0] https://www.forester-notes.org/index/index.xml

> If a tree falls in the forest...

I hope this is a pun on the content management system used to publish OP. It's forester[0], written in OCaml and parses TeX-like .tree files into semantic XML which uses browser XSLT to render the HTML.

View source on the page to get an idea.

Reminder of what the idealised web promise from decades ago was. Long gone. Very apt.

[0] https://www.forester-notes.org/index/index.xml

在 HN 中查看
kqp 2026/04/28 13:10
这不是行业建议，而是生活建议。不管你相信与否，在科技行业的趋势之外还存在着一个世界，而且这个外部世界也更大、更重要，并且被更多人认为是教育和生活的重点。

你被赋予了呼吸新鲜空气的天赋，并且只想到“好吧，这闻起来像我的房间，太糟糕了”。

It’s not industry advice, it’s life advice. Believe it or not, there exists a world outside what’s trending in the tech industry, and the outside world is also much bigger, much more important, and considered by many more to be the point of both education and life.

You were gifted a breath of fresh air, and thought only “well this does a terrible job of smelling like my room”.

lo_zamoyski 2026/04/28 09:44
教育不同于工业。教育的重点是理解和知识。产业的重点是实效和产量。目的并不相同。

您可以在不了解实例化的所有具体细节的情况下理解管理某些事物的原则。事实上，你很少这样做。

Education is distinct from industry. The point of education is understanding and knowledge. The point of industry is practical effect and production. The aims are not the same.

And you can understand the principles governing something without knowing all the concrete particulars of an instantiation. In fact, you rarely do.

在 HN 中查看
2ndorderthought 2026/04/28 09:53
我知道你在说什么。但是，我在生产中编写软件的各个团队遇到的几乎每个主要问题都需要了解所有这些细节才能解决。

我还相信在评论别人的作品之前学习基础知识是至关重要的。无论这项工作是由人还是机器完成的。

I know what you are saying. But, almost every major issue I've run into with various teams writing software in production required knowledge of all those particulars to fix.

I also believe learning the basics is essential before reviewing someone else's work. Whether that work is done by a human or machine.

在 HN 中查看
godelski 2026/04/28 09:07 

  > Who is going to read your carefully crafted documentation lol?
使用或在您的代码库中工作的每个人。

看看现在人们如何使用法学硕士。人们经常在新的代码库上使用它来加快代码的速度。坦率地说，因为它比 grep、分析和我们通常所做的所有挖掘要快得多（尽管这些仍然有好处，而且你仍然会做它们。天哪，法学硕士甚至会做这些）。但是，如果人们只花几秒钟来记录他们的代码，这在多大程度上可以避免呢？没有人说坐下来记录整个事情，而是“在添加新功能时添加一些注释”或“在您触摸的地方更新注释”。如果它花费了你超过一分钟的时间，那么你可能做错了。

我厌倦了这些争论。人们正在把小事变成大山。这实在是太近视了。我们在事情上浪费了太多他妈的时间，因为我们试图快速行动。但似乎没有人理解速度和速度之间的区别。你走得多快并不重要，重要的是速度。在错误的方向上快速前进只会伤害你，而不是帮助你。如果你没有时间知道自己是否正朝着正确的方向前进，那么你可能就错了。

  > Outside of the bit on avoiding cutting corners
但你的抱怨是什么呢 是 偷工减料。不记录？这就是偷工减料。不重构？这就是偷工减料。没有花时间在多个范围内理解代码？这就是偷工减料。

这些都是捷径，最终会浪费大量的工时。当然，它们现在可以为您节省宝贵的几秒钟或几分钟，但代价是未来几小时或几天。

事情是这样的，如果你不走这些捷径，那么这些任务都不难。甚至重构。但一旦你开始采取这些捷径，它们就会开始复合。然后一年后，您的公司正在撰写一篇博客文章，介绍您的代码如何比用 Rust（或任何酷孩子使用的任何东西）编写的代码快 500 倍。如果速度快了 500 倍，那不是因为语言发生了变化，而是因为技术债务。就像所有的债务一样，它会一点一点积累，而复利才是真正要你命的。

抱歉，我厌倦了清理每个人的烂摊子。继续前进，快速行动并打破常规。这是一种很好的学习方式（我也这样做！），但不要让别人来收拾你的烂摊子。

别再相信这种需要如此快速行动的废话了。这与诈骗者用来让您做出错误决定的反模式相同。别再欺骗自己了

  > Who is going to read your carefully crafted documentation lol?
Everyone that uses or works in your codebase.

Look at how people use LLMs these days. People frequently use it on new codebases to get up to speed on the code. Frankly because it's a lot faster than grepping, profiling, and all the digging we'd normally do (though those still have benefits and you're still going to do them. Hell, the LLMs even do them). But how much of that could have been avoided had people just taken a few seconds to document their code? No one is saying sit down and document the whole thing but "add a few comments when you add new functions" or "update comments in places you touch". If it costs you more than a minute of your time you're probably doing it wrong.

I'm tired of these arguments. People are turning molehills into mountains. It's so incredibly myopic. We waste so much fucking time on things because we're trying to move fast. But no one seems to understand the difference between speed and velocity. It never mattered how fast you go, it has always been about velocity. Going fast in the wrong direction is harming you, not helping. If you don't have the time to know if you're headed in the right direction or not then you're probably not.

  > Outside of the bit on avoiding cutting corners
But what your gripe is with is cutting corners. Not documenting? That's cutting corners. Not refactoring? That's cutting corners. Not spending time understanding the code at multiple scopes? That's cutting corners.

Those are all corners cut that end up wasting tons of man hours. Sure, they save you a few precious seconds or minutes now, but at the cost of hours or days in the future.

Here's the thing, if you don't take those shortcuts, then none of those tasks are hard. Even refactoring. But as soon as you start taking those shortcuts they start compounding. Then a year down the line your company is writing a blog post about how your code is 500x faster now that it's written in rust (or whatever the cool kids use). If it's 500x faster that's not because a language change, it's because tech debt. And like all debt it accumulates little by little and it's the compounding interest that really kills you.

Sorry, I'm tired of cleaning up everybody's messes. Go ahead, move fast and break things. It's a great way to learn (I do it too!), but don't make others clean up your mess.

Stop buying into this bullshit of needing to move so fast. It's the same anti-pattern scammers use to get you to make poor decisions. Stop scamming yourselves

在 HN 中查看
dijksterhuis 2026/04/28 10:00
这引起了我的共鸣，实际上很难。在这件事上有一句名言一直萦绕在我心头 慢即平滑，平滑即快。

再想一想，我个人更喜欢使用这个词 势头 而不是速度或简单的速度——我们积累更多 大量的 通过添加代码、功能等，并且随着质量的增加，改变方向/增加速度都变得更加困难。

this resonated for me, quite hard actually. there's the famous quote which has always stuck with me on this stuff slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

thinking about it a little more, i would personally prefer to use the term momentum rather than velocity or just plain speed -- we accrue more mass by adding code, features, etc. and shifting direction/increasing speed are both harder with greater mass.

I think mass and momentum are appropriate. I use them when talking about this too.

在 HN 中查看
stackghost 2026/04/28 09:08
这也是我在学术界的经历。 我拥有 MBA 学位（惊呼！），最好的教授都是拥有现实世界经验的人。

尽管你在 HN 评论中经常看到关于 MBA 课程如何只教毕业生如何通过热化来削减成本的常见言论，但我实际上发现这是一种很好的教育，使我成为一名更好的工程师。

反正，

最好的教授是那些在工业界工作过的人。 一位在华尔街教授金融的人喜欢区分教科书如何教授特定的技术或事实，以及从业者在现实生活中如何实际做到这一点。 一个曾经担任过风险投资家的人教了初创公司估值，一个你实际上听说过的公司的战略顾问教了竞争策略，等等。

最糟糕的教授是那些教操作的人。 他从来没有做过真正的工作。 直接从学生到助教，到博士后，再到“研究教授”，不管这意味着什么。 他所有的例子和案例研究都是无用的，或者过于简单化到了无用的地步。

TFAuthor 专注于打磨自己的技艺，这一事实表明他们完全脱离了象牙塔之外实际发生的事情。 将代码输入缓冲区从来都不是困难的部分。

This has been my experience with academia also. I have an MBA (gasp!) and the best profs were the ones who had real world experience.

Despite the common rhetoric you see in HN comments about how MBA programs only teach graduates how to cut costs by enshittifying, I actually found it a great education that made me a better engineer.

Anyway,

The best profs were the ones who'd worked in industry. One guy who taught finance worked on Wall Street and was fond of distinguishing between how the textbook taught a particular technique or fact, and how practitioners actually do it in real life. Got taught startup valuation by a guy who'd been a VC, competitive strategy by a guy who was a strategy consultant for companies you'd actually heard of, etc.

The worst profs were the ones like the guy who taught operations. He'd never worked a real job. Went straight from being a student to being a TA to a postdoc to a "research prof", whatever that means. All his examples and case studies were useless or overly simplistic to the point of being useless.

The fact that TFAuthor is concerned with polishing one's craft shows they're completely divorced from what actually happens outside the ivory tower. Typing code into a buffer has never been the hard part.

dijksterhuis 2026/04/28 08:27
> 事先有意识地决定自己的道德和伦理界限。在找到更好的东西之前，不要满足于“暂时”妥协原则的谎言。

我的英国机械工程学士学位有一个关于工程伦理的必修模块，这一直萦绕在我的脑海中。 我认为 我们一周将博帕尔灾难作为案例研究，尽管那是大约 16 年前的事了，所以我不能确定。

我很少在计算机科学系看到任何道德模块，至少在英国是这样。我认为我们总体上非常需要它们。

编辑——所以我想这是英国的事 xD 虽然我很高兴听到你们美国人也喜欢你们的道德模块

> Be intentional about deciding your own moral and ethical boundaries up front. Don't settle for the lie of compromising your principles "just for now" until you can find something better.

my uk mechanical engineering bachelors degree had a required module on the ethics of engineering which has always stuck in the back of my mind. i think we went over the bhopal disaster as a case study one week, although it was about 16 years ago now so i can't be sure.

i've rarely seen any ethics modules in computer science departments, at least here in the uk. and i think we sorely need them in general.

edit -- so i guess it's a UK thing xD though i am glad to hear that you folks in the US enjoyed your ethics modules too

在 HN 中查看
hgoel 2026/04/28 08:30
八年前，我在美国读计算机工程本科时，道德课是必修课，但 IIRC 的计算机课程没有这门课，尽管两者的职业生涯相似。不过我的记忆可能是错误的。

编辑：他们现在似乎确实有一个，所以要么是我记错了，要么是他们添加了它。

编辑 2：我记得很喜欢我的道德课，我们介绍了一些常见的例子，以及基本的合同谈判等内容。但我想我当时还没有意识到这些担忧是真实的。人们很容易相信我不会从事任何有影响力的事情。当我开始工作后，这种情况确实发生了变化。

In my computer engineering undergrad ~8 years ago in the US, an ethics class was mandatory, but IIRC the CS curriculum did not have it, despite both leading to similar careers. My memory may be wrong though.

Edit: they do seem to have one now, so either I remembered wrong or they added it.

Edit 2: I remember enjoying my ethics class, we covered some of the usual examples, and also things like basic contract negotiations. But I think I still didn't register that these concerns were real at that time. It was easy to believe that I wouldn't be working on anything that impactful. This did change once I started work.

在 HN 中查看
dijksterhuis 2026/04/28 09:20
> 但我想我当时仍然没有意识到这些担忧是真实的。人们很容易相信我不会从事任何有影响力的事情。当我开始工作后，这种情况确实发生了变化。

我提到的案例研究（可能不是博帕尔，但它绝对是基于印度发生的事情）对我来说很突出，因为它确实击中了我们最终可能做出的一些决定的影响和严重性。

还有一次，我记得讲师特意指出，某件事没有唯一正确的答案，这引起了长时间的讨论。我们必须弄清楚我们自己未来什么是对的/错的。这真的让我很困扰。

> But I think I still didn't register that these concerns were real at that time. It was easy to believe that I wouldn't be working on anything that impactful. This did change once I started work.

The case study i mentioned (it may not have been bhopal, but it was definitely based on something that happened in india) stands out for me because it really hit home about the impact and seriousness of some decisions we could end up making.

There was another time I remember the lecturer making a point of saying there was no single correct answer about something that caused a lengthy discussion. We would have to figure what's right/wrong out for ourselves going forward. That really stuck with me.

在 HN 中查看
hgoel 2026/04/28 09:35
我有不同的想法。我在纸面上理解了潜在的危害，但我认为我仍然很不成熟。我想我愿意抛开道德（例如为 Palantir 这样的公司工作）来从事有趣的前沿事物。

但当我开始工作并发现自己在做同样前沿的研究，但真正是为了公众利益时，我意识到我绝对不愿意这样抛开我的道德。也许当时我并不真正相信这是一个选择。

I was thinking about it differently. I understood the potential harm on paper, but I think I was still pretty immature. I thought I would be willing to put aside morals (eg working for companies like Palantir) to work on interesting cutting edge things.

But when I started working and found myself doing equally cutting edge research, but genuinely for the public benefit, I realized I definitely wouldn't be comfortable with putting aside my morals like that. Maybe I didn't really believe this was an option back then.

pjmorris 2026/04/28 08:49
我在教学时摘录了这些文章：

“我们应该教给我们的学生行业不想要的东西”，Kevin Ryan， https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/3377814.3381719

“你确定你的软件不会杀死任何人吗？”，Nancy Leveson， https://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/136281.2

I pull from these articles when teaching:

'We should teach our Students what Industry doesn’t want', Kevin Ryan, https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/3377814.3381719

'Are you sure your software will not kill anyone?', Nancy Leveson, https://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/136281.2

在 HN 中查看
nightpool 2026/04/28 08:30
每门 ABET 认证的计算机科学课程（我认为几乎美国的每门计算机科学课程？）都需要计算机科学道德学分。我记得复习了很多案例研究，包括 Therac 25，但我们的课程还包括很多伦理学和哲学的一般基础知识，我非常喜欢。
Every ABET accredited CS course (almost every CS course in the US I think?) requires an Ethics in Computer Science credit. I remember going over a lot of case studies, including Therac 25, but our course also included a lot of general grounding in ethics and philosophy as well, which I enjoyed a lot.

dijksterhuis 2026/04/28 08:44
啊，这很公平！也许这是/曾经是英国的事情（诚然，自从我获得硕士学位/博士学位以来，时代可能已经发生了一些变化）。

至少我现在有一篇关于 therac 25 的维基百科文章可以阅读。所以谢谢你！

另外，是的，我记得也非常喜欢道德模块。很多讨论并不总是一个明确的答案。与许多其他模块中的“一个正确的数学答案”的其余部分非常不同。

ah, fair enough! maybe it is/was a uk thing (admittedly times might have changed a little since i did my masters/phd).

at the very least i have a wikipedia article on therac 25 to read through now. so thanks for that!

also, yea i remember really enjoying the ethics module too. lots of discussion and not always a clear answer. was very different to the rest of the "one correct maths answer" in a lot of the other modules.

ciupicri 2026/04/28 09:13

在 HN 中查看
Omniusaspirer 2026/04/28 09:20
我从一个主要自学成才的软件开发人员，拥有一家小型的单人软件公司，到在美国担任护士，做出这种改变的很多动机是我想把时间花在我认为真正让世界变得更美好的工作上。科技具有令人难以置信的良好潜力，但在我看来，实际行业本身具有极其不正当的激励措施，并且没有像护理/医疗保健领域那样强大的道德基础。护士普遍认为自己是患者的倡导者，是那些经常无法表达自己意见的人的代言人。正如你可以想象的那样，这种文化并不符合现代对医疗保健利润的追求，但护士们仍在继续为正义而战。我几乎每天上班都会看到这些战斗在上演，虽然通常是专业人士完成的，但这些都是真正的工作机会之战。

在一个完美的世界中，我认为软件行业会灌输同样的美德——软件与糟糕的医疗保健一样（或更多）能够造成伤害。然而，在科技行业的推动下，我们似乎正以创纪录的速度奔向反乌托邦的未来，而我们的现代平等主义社会将无法在这一转变中幸存下来。

I went from being a largely self taught software dev with a small 1-man software business to working as a nurse in the US, and a lot of the motivation to make that change was that I wanted to spend my time doing work that I felt genuinely made the world better. Tech has incredible potential for good but the actual industry itself in my eyes has extremely perverse incentives and no strong moral foundation like that which exists in nursing/healthcare. Nurses broadly consider themselves to be patient advocates and the voice for people who often can't have their own voice. As you can imagine, this culture is not in line with the modern pursuit of healthcare profits but yet nurses stay fighting the good fight. I see these battles play out nearly every day I go to work and while it's usually done professionally these are real battles with jobs on the line.

In a perfect world I think the software industry would have instilled these same virtues- software is just as (or more) capable of causing harm as poor healthcare. Yet we seem to be racing to a dystopian future at record speed courtesy of the tech industry, and our modern egalitarian societies will not survive that transition.

在 HN 中查看
dejawu 2026/04/28 09:51
我的计算机工程学位有一门“道德”课程（实际上是一门“工程通信”课程，但它被认为满足毕业的道德要求）。这是一个关于如何归档备忘录、简历简历以及告诉招聘人员他们想听什么的学期。没有人说要考虑你受雇建造的东西的影响。当国防承包商占领了工程大楼的整个底层来举办招聘会时，我们受到鼓励去参加。

我唯一一次提到工程伦理是在应用数论（密码学）课程中，该课程由一位曾在 EFF 工作过的教授教授。他跑题告诉我们，许多问题，比如如何用导弹击中目标，可能会令我们作为工程师着迷并迫使我们，但我们不应该让这些分散我们的注意力去建造死亡工具。

那门课程是选修课，完全有可能在不提及道德的情况下完成我的学位。

我轻蔑地回顾我的学术经历有很多原因，但这个对我来说很突出。

My Computer Engineering degree had an "ethics" course (really a course on "engineering communications", but it was considered to satisfy the ethics requirement for graduation). It was a semester on how to file memos, cargo-cult your resume, and tell recruiters what they wanted to hear. Not a word was said about considering the implications of the things you're hired to build. When defense contractors took over the entire ground floor of the engineering building to hold a recruiting fair, we were encouraged to go.

The only time ethics in engineering was ever mentioned to me was in a class on applied number theory (cryptography), taught by a professor who had previously worked for the EFF. He went off-topic to tell us that many problems, like how to hit a target with a missile, may fascinate and compel us as engineers, but we shouldn't let that distract us into building instruments of death.

That course was an elective, and it was entirely possible to complete my degree without hearing a single mention of ethics.

There are many reasons I look back on my academic experience with disdain, but this one stands out to me.

在 HN 中查看
davidw 2026/04/28 09:12
90 年代并不完美，但随着开源软件的兴起，我感觉更加理想化。人们更多地考虑道德。这感觉就像是让人们能够在本地使用自己的计算机的最终浪潮的兴起，而这种浪潮已经消退了好几年了。一点云计算，现在更多的是法学硕士。如今，很多 SV 员工所拥有的公司相当糟糕。
The 90ies weren't perfect, but it felt more idealistic to me, with the rise of open source software. People thought about ethics a bit more. It felt like the ultimate tide rising to empower people locally on their own computers, and that tide has been going out for some years. A bit with cloud computing, and now a lot more with LLM's. And the company a lot of SV people keep these days is pretty gross.
在 HN 中查看
bbor 2026/04/28 08:58
FWIW：我在美国项目中有一门必修道德课（范德比尔特大学，美国南部一所富裕的私立学校）。它是所有工程师 AFAIR 的必修课程，并由工程教授教授。

体验也不错！有时会因为一般的技术道德而不是严格的职业道德而分心，但TBF这是一个非常有趣+及时的话题

FWIW: I had a mandatory ethics class in my US program (Vanderbilt, a rich private school in the American south). It was mandated for all engineers AFAIR, and taught by an engineering prof.

Pretty good experience, too! Sometimes got distracted with general tech ethics rather than strictly professional ethics, but tbf that’s a very fun+timely topic

在 HN 中查看
tptacek 2026/04/28 08:29
“我现在和将来都不会以任何形式、出于任何目的使用法学硕士。尽管从纯粹的技术角度来看，法学硕士令人着迷，但我拒绝参与或为这种建立在大规模剥削人类劳动力和挥霍稀缺资源之上的系统做出贡献。我也不认为它们实际上对人们感兴趣的许多应用程序非常有用。即使法学硕士在技术上擅长某项任务，但这并不一定意味着它们在该任务中的使用对人类做出了积极贡献。蓬勃发展。

描述我自己的一个好方法是作为一个生成人工智能素食主义者。你可以在 Sean Boots 的上述文章中找到更全面的解释，以及很多很多链接，我几乎 100% 同意。”

"I do not and will not use LLMs, in any form, for any purpose. Although LLMs are fascinating from a purely technical perspective, I refuse to participate in or contribute to such systems that are built on massive exploitation of human labor and make profligate use of scarce resources. I also don't think they are actually very good for a lot of the applications people seem excited about. Even in cases where LLMs are technically good at a task, that does not necessarily mean their use for that task contributes positively to human flourishing.

A good way to describe myself is as a generative AI vegetarian. You can find a fuller explanation—and many, many links—at the above essay by Sean Boots, which I agree with almost 100%."

在 HN 中查看
simonw 2026/04/28 08:43
我仍然希望有一天有人会培训一个法学硕士，这是持这种立场的人可以忍受的（我尊重这一立场，就像我尊重素食主义者一样，尽管我自己不是素食主义者）。

例如，我一直在跟踪完全基于不受版权保护的数据训练的模型。我还没有看到其中一个看起来普遍有用并且没有在网络上抓取或对非素食模型生成的示例进行微调的模型。

Andrej Karpathy 现在可以用不到 80 美元训练一个 GPT-2 类模型，因此至少训练的环境成本可能会下降到 LLM 素食者可以接受的程度： https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/2017703360393318587

我为什么关心？这篇文章就是一个很好的例子。如果你是一名计算机科学教授，我真的希望你能够在不违反你的原则的情况下修改这一类令人着迷的模型。

更新：嗯，说到潜在的素食模特，我刚刚看到 https://talkie-lm.com/introducing-talkie 在 HN 主页上 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47927903

我之前探索过不同的不受版权保护的训练模型 Mr Chatterbox，但发现它在 Haiku 和 GPT-4o-mini 的合成对话对的帮助下受到了轻微损坏 - https://simonwillison.net/2026/Mar/30/mr-chatterbox/

不过，Talkie 也不完全纯粹：“最后，我们进行了另一轮监督微调，这次是在 Claude Opus 4.6 和 talkie 之间的拒绝采样多轮合成聊天中，以消除其对话能力中持续存在的粗糙边缘。”

I remain hopeful that some day someone will train an LLM which is tolerable to people who take this stance (which I respect, much like I respect food vegetarians despite not being one myself).

I've been tracking models trained entirely on out-of-copyright data, for example. I've not yet seen one of those which appears generally useful and didn't chuck in a scrape of the web or get fine-tuned on examples generated by a non-vegetarian model.

Andrej Karpathy can train a GPT-2 class model for less than $80 now, so at least the environmental cost of training may drop to a point that it's acceptable to LLM vegetarians: https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/2017703360393318587

Why do I care? This post is a great example. If you're a professor of computer science I really want you to be able to tinker with this fascinating class of models without violating your principles.

UPDATE: Huh, speaking of potentially vegetarian models, I just saw https://talkie-lm.com/introducing-talkie on the HN homepage https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47927903

I've explored I different out-of-copyright trained model Mr Chatterbox before but found it to have been mildly corrupted through the help of synthetic conversation pairs from Haiku and GPT-4o-mini - https://simonwillison.net/2026/Mar/30/mr-chatterbox/

Talkie isn't entirely pure either though: "Finally, we did another round of supervised fine-tuning, this time on rejection-sampled multi-turn synthetic chats between Claude Opus 4.6 and talkie, to smooth out persistent rough edges in its conversational abilities."

我不明白为什么你和这个评论部分的其他人很难理解为什么人们如此讨厌人工智能，因为这不仅仅是盗窃和环境破坏。大学教授，尤其是文理学院的教授，显然不会喜欢让你外包思维并窃取你的代理权的东西。我认为这是一个完全正确的观点；也许可以和住在旧金山以外、没有 STEM 大脑的人谈谈。

A college professor, especially one at a liberal arts school, is obviously not going to like something that enables you to outsource your thinking and steals your agency. I think that's a perfectly valid viewpoint; maybe talk to someone without STEM-brain who lives outside of SF for once.

infotainment 2026/04/28 08:51
> Andrej Karpathy 现在可以用不到 80 美元训练一个 GPT-2 类模型，因此至少训练的环境成本可能会下降到 LLM 素食者可以接受的程度： https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/2017703360393318587

我怀疑，即使你降低了培训成本或任何其他现实世界的指标，目标也会立即移动。在我看来，这从来都不是关于那些事情，而只是关于通过避开被视为趋势的东西而获得的优越感。

> Andrej Karpathy can train a GPT-2 class model for less than $80 now, so at least the environmental cost of training may drop to a point that it's acceptable to LLM vegetarians: https://twitter.com/karpathy/status/2017703360393318587

I suspect that even if you reduced the cost of training or any other real world metric, the goalposts would immediately move. It seems to me that it has never been about those things, but simply about the feeling of superiority one can attain by eschewing something seen as trending.

infotainment 2026/04/28 08:35
> 建立在对人类劳动力的大规模剥削和对稀缺资源的挥霍之上

在我看来，网上仇恨者无限重复的这种夸张说法是没有建设性的。我会 相当 可以肯定的是，与训练机器学习模型相比，作者访问互联网的任何计算设备的制造都使用了更多的资源并利用了更多的人力。

> built on massive exploitation of human labor and make profligate use of scarce resources

This kind of hyperbole repeated ad infinitum by haters online is not-constructive, IMO. I would be quite certain that the manufacture of whatever computing device the author is accessing the internet on used far more resources and exploited far more human labor than training an ML model ever did.

wanderingmind 2026/04/28 09:03
没有对这个人的不尊重，但这似乎是由一个一生都在学术泡沫中度过的人写的，而不必与具有不同兴趣的人和实体打交道，也不必与时间对任何决定的影响打交道。我相信许多艺术家会喜欢根据自己的主观兴趣花更多的时间来完善他们的艺术。然而，如果他们优先考虑这一点，而不了解客户的需求，他们就会破产。通过你的爱好培养你的兴趣。如果它们与赚钱能力相符，那么您就是少数幸运者之一。对于绝大多数人来说，他们并不一致。
No disrespect to the person, but this seems to be written by someone who has spent their life in academic bubble, without having to deal with people and entities with diverging interests and the impact of time on any decisions. I'm sure many artists will love to spend more time perfecting their art, based on their subjective interests. However, if they prioritize that, without understanding what their customer wants, they will go bankrupt. Nourish your interests through your hobbies. If they align with money making capability, you are one of the lucky few. For a significant majority, they do not align.

jmward01 2026/04/28 08:32
'培养你深入思考的能力。尽一切努力在空间和时间上为自己创造一个不受干扰的泡泡。”

我发现当我重新开始锻炼和阅读时，我的生活就会变得井井有条。这些事情我从来没有足够的时间去做，直到我开始定期做这些事情，此时我意识到它们实际上使我有更多的时间做事情，而不是更少。

'Cultivate your ability to think deeply. Do whatever it takes to carve out distraction-free bubbles for yourself in both space and time.'

I find that when I get back into exercise and reading so much more of my life falls into place. These are things that I never have enough time for until I start doing them regularly at which point I realize that they actually enable me to have more time to do things, not less.

_jackdk_ 2026/04/28 08:35
Yorgey 教授多年来做了一些伟大的工作，并写了我最喜欢的论文之一*。他能这样说话真是太好了。不久前，我在母校看到一位来自 Anthropic 的工程师演讲，这次会议给我留下的压倒性印象是：“如果 Anthropic 注定是优秀的，那么我们真的会遇到困难。”

* 幺半群：主题和变体（功能性珍珠）： http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/pub/monoid-pearl.pdf

Prof. Yorgey has done some great work over the years, and wrote one of my favourite papers*. Good on him for speaking up like this. I saw an engineer from Anthropic speak at my alma mater a little while ago and the overwhelming impression I took away from the session was, "if Anthropic are meant to be the good ones, we're really going to be in for a rough time."

* Monoids: Theme and variations (functional pearl): http://ozark.hendrix.edu/~yorgey/pub/monoid-pearl.pdf

cdot2 2026/04/28 08:48
“技术被用来分散注意力、提取、监视和杀戮”

第一台通用可编程计算机于 1945 年设计，用于计算美国陆军的火炮发射表，并立即用于帮助设计核武器。计算机和所有技术一直并将永远被用作武器（直接或间接）。

"where technology is used to distract, extract, surveil, and kill"

The first general purpose, programmable computer was designed in 1945 to calculate artillery firing tables for the US Army and was immediately used to help design nuclear weapons. Computers and all technology has always been, and will always be, used as a weapon (either directly or indirectly).

MattyRad 2026/04/28 13:29
请参阅下面的内容：

不要相信关于技术“不可避免”或“将继续存在”的自私谎言。你不必仅仅跟随主流叙事。你可以做出深思熟虑的选择，并帮助其他人也这样做。”

See below that:

'Don't believe self-serving lies about technologies being "inevitable" or "here to stay". You don't have to just go along with the dominant narrative. You can make deliberate choices and help others to do the same.'

torben-friis 2026/04/28 08:17
>培养你深入思考的能力。尽一切努力在空间和时间上为自己创造一个不受干扰的泡泡。这可能意味着对其他人认为至关重要或不可避免的技术或工作模式说不。

目前正在为实现这一目标而努力奋斗。我们都知道现在一切都在争夺我们的注意力，但我可以向你保证，除非你积极地尝试与之抗争，否则你不会知道这种情况发生的程度。

>Cultivate your ability to think deeply. Do whatever it takes to carve out distraction-free bubbles for yourself in both space and time. This might mean saying no to technologies or patterns of working that others say are critical or inevitable.

Currently struggling hard to achieve this. We all know everything fights for our attention nowadays, but I can assure you that you don't have an idea of the degree this happens until you actively try to fight it.

在 HN 中查看
vitacoco 2026/04/28 08:37
“我现在不会也不会以任何形式、出于任何目的使用法学硕士。” 学术的肚脐凝视在这一点上很强烈。
"I do not and will not use LLMs, in any form, for any purpose." The academic navel gazing is strong with this one.
在 HN 中查看
arcfour 2026/04/28 12:05
我不知道为什么现在一切都必须如此两极分化。你可以使用法学硕士，而不会让你变成白痴、妄想症、精神病或其他什么。他们有问题吗？当然。是否有人和组织过于依赖它们？无疑。认识到它们具有实用性并将它们用作你的武器库中的另一种工具是否意味着你认为它们是绝对正确的并且是你自己思考能力的合适替代品？显然不是。

实在是太累了。现在，你甚至无法谈论该技术的优缺点，人们会立即跳到各自的一边。它要么必须全部好，要么全部坏。不允许有任何细微差别。

文章作者的立场太过极端，让人觉得他们无知又愚蠢。我希望我的老师更加开放/好奇，并且有更细致的意见。

I don't know why everything has to be so polarized these days. You can use LLMs without it making you an idiot or delusional or psychotic or something. Are there issues with them? Of course. Are there people and organizations that rely on them too heavily? Without a doubt. Does recognizing that they have utility and using them as another tool in your arsenal mean you think they're infallible and are a suitable substitute for your own capacity to think? Obviously not.

It's really tiring. You can't even talk about the pros and cons of the technology now without people immediately jumping to their respective sides over it. It either has to be all good or all bad. No nuance allowed.

The position espoused by the author of the article is so extreme it makes them look ignorant and foolish. I would want my teacher to be more open-minded/curious and have a more nuanced opinion than that.

在 HN 中查看
hgoel 2026/04/28 08:39
我同意所提出的观点，即使我个人同意法学硕士（只要谨慎使用）。

我认为这对学生尤其重要，因为他们依赖法学硕士对自己的伤害最大。就像年幼的孩子被迫用手做数学而不是使用计算器来建立直觉和记忆一样，学生应该致力于手动做事来培养他们的技能。

去制作那个玩具网站、游戏、操作系统、模拟器或编程语言。阅读规范并尝试自己实施。您所处的环境并不需要您大量开发功能，您可以探索！

I agree with the points made, even if personally I am okay with LLMs (as long as they're used with appropriate caution).

Especially relevant for students I think, since they are hurting themselves most by relying on LLMs. Just like how young children are forced to do math by hand instead of using calculators to build intuition and memory, students should aim to do things manually to build their skills.

Go make that toy website, game, OS, emulator or programming language. Read specifications and try implementing them yourself. You aren't in an environment that requires you to churn out features, you can explore!

在 HN 中查看
Trav5 2026/04/28 10:40
我完全同意并且最近一直在思考这个问题。我的儿子一直在一个有趣的业余项目上手动完成大量工程工作，包括 3D 设计、打印、焊接等。但他使用 Ai 来对 arduino 进行编程。我希望他理解代码，但我正在努力找到学校课程与该项目所需的所有其他内容之间的平衡。他现在没有时间深入研究这一切。这个项目给了他机会，但也许编码还不需要手动完成。
I totally agree and have been thinking about this lately. My son has been been doing an incredible amount of engineering work on a fun side project manually including 3D design and printing, soldering etc. But he used Ai to program an arduino. I want him to understand the code but I'm trying to find the balance between the school classes, and all the other things needed for this project. He doesn't have time dive into it all right now. This project is giving him exposure but maybe the coding doesn't need to be done manually just yet.

Yeah, I can imagine that it's tricky to find a balance when so many new things are put together.

I didn't have the benefit of skilled teachers or parents that believed in my interests (and of course no LLMs!) so I didn't have a choice besides learning to deal with the frustration and getting good at scouring docs/code.

在 HN 中查看
testermelon 2026/04/28 08:36
我真的很喜欢这种鼓励。老实说，它引起了我很多共鸣。说明手艺本身还是很美的，只是需要找到合适的人交往而已。

但现实世界和金钱的混合创造了一种奇怪的腐败混合物，就像一切一样。更不用说那些已经在这个行业站稳脚跟但还没有足够的资历来生存或控制人工智能替代品的人面临着真正的风险。而且很有可能，所需的资历比人们想象的要高得多。最终，经济驱动力才是主导力量。

I really love the encouragement. Honestly it resonates a lot with me. It shows that the craft itself is still beautiful, you just need to find the right people to mingle with.

But the real world and money blended in creates a weird corrupt mix, just like everything. Not to mention there is a real risk for people who are already has their feet in the industry but not yet senior enough to survive or to control, for example, the AI replacements. And more than likely, the seniority required is way higher than one would think. In the end, economic drives are the dominant forces.

在 HN 中查看
lo_zamoyski 2026/04/28 10:02
事实是，当你可以自由地从事与知识有关的学术项目时，“手艺”是美丽的。实际上，工业和商业代码很少那么漂亮。看看提供的开发工具旨在控制丑陋并帮助管理混乱。在这方面我有点老派，但一段时间以来，许多开发人员依赖各种工具来编写代码，这些工具消除了与手动编程过程的接触等。

区分实践和理论很重要。 “理想主义者”轻率的回答拒绝涉及混乱的事实领域，因为这在审美上是令人反感的，或者挑战了他们的舒适感或怀旧感。蒸汽机也不是不可避免的，但人们确实选择了它。今天，这个论坛上有多少人抱怨蒸汽机取代旧的工作方式时世界的消失？下一代人不会再遇到这些问题，就像我们在蒸汽机方面不会遇到这些问题一样。或者，如果您愿意的话，有多少人渴望汇编编程的日子？

当某件事被证明在工业上太有用而无法选择退出时，它就会被采用。人们会选择它。如果你想成为阿米什人，就去吧，但大多数人不这样做。

The fact of the matter is that "the craft" is beautiful when you are free to work on academic projects that are concerned with knowledge. In practice, industrial and commercial code is rarely that beautiful. Look at the offering of dev tools designed to reign in the ugliness and help manage the chaos. I'm sort of old school in this regard, but for some time now, many devs rely on all sorts of tooling to write the code, tooling that removes a layer of contact with manual processes of programming and so forth.

It's important to distinguish between the practical and the theoretical. The flippant answers of "idealists" refuse to engage with the messy domain of facts, because it is aesthetically offensive or challenges their comfort or their nostalgia. The steam engine wasn't inevitable either, but people did choose it. How many today in this forum grumble about the loss of a world when the steam engine replaced old ways of working? The next generation won't have these sorts of hangups, just as we don't have them about steam engines. Or, if you like, how many pine for the days of assembly programming?

When something proves to be too useful industrially to opt out of, then it will be adopted. People will choose it. If you want to be Amish, go for it, but most people don't.

2ndorderthought 2026/04/28 09:16
通过了解计算机并享受您所在的领域，您将比那些说“测试通过”、“在我的机器上工作”、“也许在我公司的实时产品数据库上运行代理是个好主意”的人更加熟练。任何人都可以学会如何改进，包括那些热衷于编写代码作为爱好的人。

无论如何，并不是所有事情都是为了赚钱。

By understanding computers and enjoying the field you are in you will be more skilled then someone who says "tests pass", "worked on my machine", "maybe it's a good idea to run agents on my companies live prod database". Anyone can learn to slop it up, including someone who is passionate about writing code as a hobby.

Not everything is about making money anyways.

在 HN 中查看
archievillain 2026/04/28 15:46
这个人是勒德分子。我只是认为这并不意味着 HN 上的大多数人希望它意味着什么，正如阅读实际文章所显示的那样。你甚至不需要让你选择的法学硕士为你总结，因为要点包含在前两段中：第一段，卢德分子是抗议他们恶劣的生活条件的工人。第二段，这些工人被政府监禁并杀害。

然后，在文章的下面，它详细阐述了：

> 勒德运动是在拿破仑战争的恶劣经济气候下兴起的，当时英格兰和威尔士新建纺织工厂的工作条件日益艰苦，出生率不断下降，教育水平不断提高。

> 勒德分子并不反对使用机器本身（许多人是纺织行业的熟练操作员）；他们攻击那些试图规避当时标准劳工惯例的制造商。

>危机引发了广泛的抗议和暴力，但中产阶级和上层阶级强烈支持政府，政府动用军队镇压所有工人阶级的骚乱，特别是卢德运动。

This person is a Luddite. I just don't think that implies what most people on HN wish it would imply, though, as reading thea actual article shows. You don't even need to ask your LLM of choice to summarize it for you, as the salient point is contained within the first two paragraphs: paragraph one, the Luddites were workers protesting their terrible living conditions. Paragraph two, these workers were jailed and killed by the government.

Then, further down the article, it elaborates:

> The Luddite movement emerged during the harsh economic climate of the Napoleonic Wars, which saw a rise in difficult working conditions in the new textile factories paired with decreasing birth rates and a rise in education standards in England and Wales.

> Luddites were not opposed to the use of machines per se (many were skilled operators in the textile industry); they attacked manufacturers who were trying to circumvent standard labour practices of the time.

>The crisis led to widespread protest and violence, but the middle classes and upper classes strongly supported the government, which used the army to suppress all working-class unrest, especially the Luddite movement.

danny_codes 2026/04/28 08:44
这是一个疲倦、软弱、可悲的论点。如果技术弊大于利，那么反对技术就是非常合理的。

就当今的法学硕士而言，绝大多数公众认为它们弊大于利。

为什么要接受生活质量下降而不是合理的监管？

This is a tired, weak, and pathetic argument. Opposition to technology is very reasonable if that technology is doing more harm than good.

In the case of present-day LLMs, the vast majority of the public finds them to be more harmful than beneficial.

Why accept a decreasing quality of live instead of sensible regulation?

在 HN 中查看
xtracto 2026/04/28 14:06
我理解你的观点，并且清楚地看到法学硕士不能与音频相比……但是……

当我还是个孩子的时候，音乐、音频和音响系统都以高质量为标准。

如今，人们主要使用蓝牙耳机听音乐，蓝牙耳机基本上重新压缩已经压缩的音频信号，以低质量发送它们。此外，找到播放高质量音乐的好音响也越来越困难。要么，您必须为价格过高的“发烧友”设备支付非常高的价格，要么您只能使用廉价的中国 MP3 播放器。

然而，社会和市场已经发声。有时，社会很乐意接受稍差的产品以换取价格和便利。

I understand your point and clearly see that LLMs cannot be compared to audio ... but ...

Back when I was a kid, music, audio and sound systems had high quality as a standard.

Nowadays people listen to music mostly with bluetooth headphones which basically recompress an already compressed audio signal to send them in low quality. Also, it is more and more difficult to find OK stereos that play music in good quality. Either, you have to pay very high prices for overpriced "audiophile" equipment, or you are stuck with cheap chinese MP3 players.

Yet, society and markets have spoken. Sometimes society is happy to accept marginally worse products in exchange of price and convenience.

GaryBluto 2026/04/28 08:53
> 绝大多数公众认为它们弊大于利。

大多数人一度持有的荒谬和错误信念的例子：

- 自发发电

- “瘴气”导致疾病

- 地球位于宇宙的中心

- 心是思想的所在地，大脑是无用的

- 寒冷的天气导致感冒

永远不要相信“绝大多数”能把事情做好。

> the vast majority of the public finds them to be more harmful than beneficial.

Examples of ridiculous and incorrect beliefs once held by majorities:

- Spontaneous generation

- "Miasma" causes disease

- Earth is at the centre of the universe

- The heart is the seat of thought and the brain is useless

- Cold weather causes colds

Don't trust "the vast majority" to get anything right, ever.

"Won't somebody think of the children" is constantly used sarcastically in order to dismiss the concerns of people who want to ban something they claim is harmful to children.
